highlighted_text: Java allows for functional programming within a class, but object-oriented is still the main organization of code.

highlighted_text: Platform Independent Java is an interpreted language that gets compiled to bytecode. A key benefit is that Java code gets compiled once rather than needing to be recompiled for different operating systems. This is known as “write once, run everywhere.”
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Robust One of the major advantages of Java over C++ is that it prevents memory leaks. Java manages memory on its own and does garbage collection automatically.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Secure Java code runs inside the JVM. This creates a sandbox that makes it hard for Java code to do evil things to the computer it is running on

highlighted_text: Multithreaded Java is designed to allow multiple pieces of code to run at the same time.

Order for declaring a class

Element	Example	Required?	Where does it go?
Package declaration	package abc;	No	First line in the file
Import statements	import java.util.*;	No	Immediately after the package (if present)
Class declaration	public class C	Yes	Immediately after the import (if any)
Field declarations	int value;	No	Any top-level element in a class
Method declarations	void method()	No	Any top-level element in a class

highlighted_text:
Summary
The Java Development Kit (JDK) is used to do software development. It contains the compiler (javac), which turns source code into bytecode. It also contains the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) launcher (java), which launches the JVM and then calls the code. Application programming interfaces (APIs) are available to call reusable pieces of code.

Java code is object-oriented, meaning all code is defined in classes. Access modifiers allow classes to encapsulate data. Java is platform independent, compiling to bytecode. It is robust and simple by not providing pointers or operator overloading. Java is secure because it runs inside a virtual machine. Finally, the language facilitates multithreaded programming and strives for backward compatibility.

Java classes consist of members called fields and methods. An object is an instance of a Java class. There are three styles of comments: a single-line comment (//), a multiline comment (/* */), and a Javadoc comment (/** */).

Java begins program execution with a main() method. The most common signature for this method run from the command line is public static void main(String[] args). Arguments are passed in after the class name, as in java NameOfClass firstArgument. Arguments are indexed starting with 0.

Java code is organized into folders called packages. To reference classes in other packages, you use an import statement. A wildcard ending an import statement means you want to import all classes in that package. It does not include packages that are inside that one. The package java.lang is special in that it does not need to be imported.

For some class elements, order matters within the file. The package statement comes first if present. Then come the import statements if present. Then comes the class declaration. Fields and methods are allowed to be in any order within the class.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Java allows operator overloading.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Which of the following are true statements? (Choose all that apply.)
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Exam Essentials
Identify benefits of Java. Benefits of Java include object-oriented design, encapsulation, platform independence, robustness, simplicity, security, multithreading, and backward compatibility.

Define common acronyms. The JDK stands for Java Development Kit and contains the compiler and JVM launcher. The JVM stands for Java Virtual Machine, and it runs bytecode. API is an application programming interface, which is code that you can call.

Be able to write code using a main() method. A main() method is usually written as public static void main(String[] args). Arguments are referenced starting with args[0]. Accessing an argument that wasn’t passed in will cause the code to throw an exception.

Understand the effect of using packages and imports. Packages contain Java classes. Classes can be imported by class name or wildcard. Wildcards do not look at subdirectories. In the event of a conflict, class name imports take precedence.

Be able to recognize misplaced statements in a class. Package and import statements are optional. If present, both go before the class declaration in that order. Fields and methods are also optional and are allowed in any order within the class declaration.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

CALLING CONSTRUCTORS
To create an instance of a class, all you have to do is write new before the class name and add parentheses after it.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: It’s called a constructor, which is a special type of method that creates a new object.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: There are two key points to note about the constructor: the name of the constructor matches the name of the class, and there’s no return type.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The purpose of a constructor is to initialize fields, although you can put any code in there.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Another way to initialize fields is to do so directly on the line on which they’re declared.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: For most classes, you don’t have to code a constructor—the compiler will supply a “do nothing” default constructor for you.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: READING AND WRITING MEMBER FIELDS
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Park p = new Park();
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: EXECUTING INSTANCE INITIALIZER BLOCKS
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: When you learned about methods, you saw braces ({}). The code between the braces (sometimes called “inside the braces”) is called a code block. Anywhere you see braces is a code block.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Sometimes code blocks are inside a method. These are run when the method is called. Other times, code blocks appear outside a method. These are called instance initializers.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: FOLLOWING ORDER OF INITIALIZATION
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Fields and instance initializer blocks are run in the order in which they appear in the file.
The constructor runs after all fields and instance initializer blocks have run.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
{ System.out.println(name); }  // DOES NOT COMPILE
private String name = "Fluffy";
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Order matters for the fields and blocks of code. You can’t refer to a variable before it has been defined:
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Understanding Data Types
Java applications contain two types of data: primitive types and reference types.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
USING PRIMITIVE TYPES
Java has eight built-in data types, referred to as the Java primitive types. These eight data types represent the building blocks for Java objects, because all Java objects are just a complex collection of these primitive data types. That said, a primitive is not an object in Java nor does it represent an object. A primitive is just a single value in memory, such as a number or character.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Primitive types

Keyword	Type	Example
boolean	true or false	true
byte	8-bit integral value	123
short	16-bit integral value	123
int	32-bit integral value	123
long	64-bit integral value	123L
float	32-bit floating-point value	123.45f
double	64-bit floating-point value	123.456
char	16-bit Unicode value	'a'
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
IS STRING A PRIMITIVE?
No, it is not.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
The float and double types are used for floating-point (decimal) values.
A float requires the letter f following the number so Java knows it is a float.
The byte, short, int, and long types are used for numbers without decimal points. In mathematics, these are all referred to as integral values, but in Java, int and Integer refer to specific types.
Each numeric type uses twice as many bits as the smaller similar type. For example, short uses twice as many bits as byte does.
All of the numeric types are signed in Java. This means that they reserve one of their bits to cover a negative range. For example, byte ranges from -128 to 127. You might be surprised that the range is not -128 to 128. Don’t forget, 0 needs to be accounted for too in the range.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: you should be aware that short and char are closely related, as both are stored as integral types with the same 16-bit length. The primary difference is that short is signed, which means it splits its range across the positive and negative integers. Alternatively, char is unsigned, which means range is strictly positive including 0. Therefore, char can hold a higher positive numeric value than short, but cannot hold any negative numbers.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: SIGNED AND UNSIGNED: SHORT AND CHAR
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: FLOATING-POINT NUMBERS AND SCIENTIFIC NOTATION
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: In most computer systems, floating-point numbers are stored in scientific notation. This means the numbers are stored as two numbers, a and b, of the form a x 10b.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Writing Literals
There are a few more things you should know about numeric primitives. When a number is present in the code, it is called a literal.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: By default, Java assumes you are defining an int value with a numeric literal.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Octal (digits 0–7), which uses the number 0 as a prefix—for example, 017
Hexadecimal (digits 0–9 and letters A–F/a–f), which uses 0x or 0X as a prefix—for example, 0xFF, 0xff, 0XFf. Hexadecimal is case insensitive so all of these examples mean the same value.
Binary (digits 0–1), which uses the number 0 followed by b or B as a prefix—for example, 0b10, 0B10
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: long max = 3123456789;  // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: long max = 3123456789L;  // now Java knows it is a long
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: You can add underscores anywhere except at the beginning of a literal, the end of a literal, right before a decimal point, or right after a decimal point.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
double notAtStart = _1000.00;          // DOES NOT COMPILE
double notAtEnd = 1000.00_;            // DOES NOT COMPILE
double notByDecimal = 1000_.00;        // DOES NOT COMPILE
double annoyingButLegal = 1_00_0.0_0;  // Ugly, but compiles
double reallyUgly = 1__________2;      // Also compiles
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
USING REFERENCE TYPES
A reference type refers to an object (an instance of a class). Unlike primitive types that hold their values in the memory where the variable is allocated, references do not hold the value of the object they refer to. Instead, a reference “points” to an object by storing the memory address where the object is located, a concept referred to as a pointer.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
A value is assigned to a reference in one of two ways:

A reference can be assigned to another object of the same or compatible type.
A reference can be assigned to a new object using the new keyword.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: DISTINGUISHING BETWEEN PRIMITIVES AND REFERENCE TYPES
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: First, reference types can be assigned null, which means they do not currently refer to an object. Primitive types will give you a compiler error if you attempt to assign them null.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
int value = null;   // DOES NOT COMPILE
String s = null;
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Next, reference types can be used to call methods, assuming the reference is not null. Primitives do not have methods declared on them.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Declaring Variables
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: A variable is a name for a piece of memory that stores data.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
String zooName;
int numberAnimals;
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Now that we’ve declared a variable, we can give it a value. This is called initializing a variable. To initialize a variable, you just type the variable name followed by an equal sign
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
zooName = "The Best Zoo";
numberAnimals = 100;
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
String zooName = "The Best Zoo";
int numberAnimals = 100;
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
IDENTIFYING IDENTIFIERS
It probably comes as no surprise to you that Java has precise rules about identifier names. An identifier is the name of a variable, method, class, interface, or package.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
There are only four rules to remember for legal identifiers:

Identifiers must begin with a letter, a $ symbol, or a _ symbol.
Identifiers can include numbers but not start with them.
Since Java 9, a single underscore _ is not allowed as an identifier.
You cannot use the same name as a Java reserved word. A reserved word is special word that Java has held aside so that you are not allowed to use it. Remember that Java is case sensitive, so you can use versions of the keywords that only differ in case. Please don’t, thoug
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: h.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Reserved words

abstract	assert	boolean	break	byte
case	catch	char	class	const*
continue	default	do	double	else
enum	extends	false**	final	finally
float	for	goto*	if	implements
import	instanceof	int	interface	long
native	new	null**	package	private
protected	public	return	short	static
strictfp	super	switch	synchronized	this
throw	throws	transient	true**	try
void	volatile	while	_ (underscore)
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
int 3DPointClass;    // identifiers cannot begin with a number
byte hollywood@vine; // @ is not a letter, digit, $ or _
String *$coffee;     // * is not a letter, digit, $ or _
double public;       // public is a reserved word
short _;             // a single underscore is not allowed
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: These examples are not legal:
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The following examples are legal:
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Style: camelCase
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: the first letter of each word is capitalized.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Style: snake_case
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: It simply uses an underscore (_) to separate words, often entirely in lowercase.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
DECLARING MULTIPLE VARIABLES
You can also declare and initialize multiple variables in the same statement.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: int num, String value; // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: If you want to declare multiple variables in the same statement, they must share the same type declaration and not repeat it.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Initializing Variables
Before you can use a variable, it needs a value. Some types of variables get this value set automatically, and others require the programmer to specify it.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
CREATING LOCAL VARIABLES
A local variable is a variable defined within a constructor, method, or initializer block.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Local variables do not have a default value and must be initialized before use.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
PASSING CONSTRUCTOR AND METHOD PARAMETERS
Variables passed to a constructor or method are called constructor parameters or method parameters, respectively.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: These parameters are local variables that have been pre-initialized. In other words, they are like local variables that have been initialized before the method is called, by the caller.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: public void findAnswer(boolean check) {}
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
DEFINING INSTANCE AND CLASS VARIABLES
Variables that are not local variables are defined either as instance variables or as class variables. An instance variable, often called a field, is a value defined within a specific instance of an object.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: On the other hand, a class variable is one that is defined on the class level and shared among all instances of the class. It can even be publicly accessible to classes outside the class without requiring an instance to use.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: You can tell a variable is a class variable because it has the keyword static before it.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Instance and class variables do not require you to initialize them. As soon as you declare these variables, they are given a default value.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Default initialization values by type

Variable type	Default initialization value
boolean	false
byte, short, int, long	0
float, double	0.0
char	'\u0000' (NUL)
All object references (everything else)	null
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
INTRODUCING VAR
Starting in Java 10, you have the option of using the keyword var instead of the type for local variables under certain conditions.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The formal name of this feature is local variable type inference.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Type Inference of var
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: When you type var, you are instructing the compiler to determine the type for you. The compiler looks at the code on the line of the declaration and uses it to infer the type.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: All the types declared on a single line must be the same type and share the same declaration.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Examples with var
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: var a = 2, b = 3;  // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Java does not allow var in multiple variable declarations.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
VAR AND NULL
While a var cannot be initialized with a null value without a type, it can be assigned a null value after it is declared, provided that the underlying data type of the var is an object.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: a and b are method parameters. These are not local variables.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Naming a local variable var is legal.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: There’s one last rule you should be aware of. While var is not a reserved word and allowed to be used as an identifier, it is considered a reserved type name. A reserved type name means it cannot be used to define a type, such as a class, interface, or enum.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Review of var Rules
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
A var is used as a local variable in a constructor, method, or initializer block.
A var cannot be used in constructor parameters, method parameters, instance variables, or class variables.
A var is always initialized on the same line (or statement) where it is declared.
The value of a var can change, but the type cannot.
A var cannot be initialized with a null value without a type.
A var is not permitted in a multiple-variable declaration.
A var is a reserved type name but not a reserved word, meaning it can be used as an identifier except as a class, interface, or enum name.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Managing Variable Scope
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: There are two local variables in this method. The bitesOfCheese variable is declared inside the method. The piecesOfCheese variable is a method parameter and, as discussed earlier, it also acts like a local variable in terms of garbage collection and scope. Both of these variables are said to have a scope local to the method. This means they cannot be used outside of where they are defined.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
LIMITING SCOPE
Local variables can never have a scope larger than the method they are defined in. However, they can have a smaller scope.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
NESTING SCOPE
Remember that blocks can contain other blocks. These smaller contained blocks can reference variables defined in the larger scoped blocks, but not vice versa.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: APPLYING SCOPE TO CLASSES
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Luckily the rule for instance variables is easier: they are available as soon as they are defined and last for the entire lifetime of the object itself. The rule for class, aka static, variables is even easier: they go into scope when declared like the other variable types. However, they stay in scope for the entire life of the program.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: REVIEWING SCOPE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
the rules on scope:

Local variables: In scope from declaration to end of block
Instance variables: In scope from declaration until object eligible for garbage collection
Class variables: In scope from declaration until program ends
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Destroying Objects
Now that we’ve played with our objects, it is time to put them away. Luckily, the JVM automatically takes care of that for you. Java provides a garbage collector to automatically look for objects that aren’t needed anymore.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: All Java objects are stored in your program memory’s heap. The heap, which is also referred to as the free store, represents a large pool of unused memory allocated to your Java application. The heap may be quite large, depending on your environment, but there is always a limit to its size.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
UNDERSTANDING GARBAGE COLLECTION
Garbage collection refers to the process of automatically freeing memory on the heap by deleting objects that are no longer reachable in your program.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Eligible for Garbage Collection
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: In Java and other languages, eligible for garbage collection refers to an object’s state of no longer being accessible in a program and therefore able to be garbage collected.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Does this mean an object that’s eligible for garbage collection will be immediately garbage collected? Definitely not.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: When the object actually is discarded is not under your control
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: When is System.gc() guaranteed to be called by the JVM? Never, actually.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: TRACING ELIGIBILITY
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
The JVM waits patiently and monitors each object until it determines that the code no longer needs that memory. An object will remain on the heap until it is no longer reachable. An object is no longer reachable when one of two situations occurs:

The object no longer has any references pointing to it.
All references to the object have gone out of scope.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: OBJECTS VS. REFERENCES
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The reference is a variable that has a name and can be used to access the contents of an object. A reference can be assigned to another reference, passed to a method, or returned from a method. All references are the same size, no matter what their type is.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: An object sits on the heap and does not have a name. Therefore, you have no way to access an object except through a reference. Objects come in all different shapes and sizes and consume varying amounts of memory. An object cannot be assigned to another object, and an object cannot be passed to a method or returned from a method. It is the object that gets garbage collected, not its reference.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: _helloWorld$
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Public
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Brand = null
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: var fall = "leaves";

highlighted_text: Understanding Java Operators
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: A Java operator is a special symbol that can be applied to a set of variables, values, or literals—referred to as operands—and that returns a result.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: TYPES OF OPERATORS
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: In general, three flavors of operators are available in Java: unary, binary, and ternary. These types of operators can be applied to one, two, or three operands, respectively.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Java operators are not necessarily evaluated from left-to-right order
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: OPERATOR PRECEDENCE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: In mathematics, certain operators can override other operators and be evaluated first. Determining which operators are evaluated in what order is referred to as operator precedence.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Order of operator precedence

Operator	Symbols and examples
Post-unary operators	expression++, expression--
Pre-unary operators	++expression, --expression
Other unary operators	-, !, ~, +, (type)
Multiplication/division/modulus	*, /, %
Addition/subtraction	+, -
Shift operators	<<, >>, >>>
Relational operators	<, >, <=, >=, instanceof
Equal to/not equal to	==, !=
Logical operators	&, ^, |
Short-circuit logical operators	&&, ||
Ternary operators	boolean expression ? expression1 : expression2
Assignment operators	=, +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, ^=, |=, <<=, >>=, >>>=
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Applying Unary Operators
By definition, a unary operator is one that requires exactly one operand, or variable, to function.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Unary operators

Operator	Description
!	Inverts a boolean’s logical value
+	Indicates a number is positive, although numbers are assumed to be positive in Java unless accompanied by a negative unary operator
-	Indicates a literal number is negative or negates an expression
++	Increments a value by 1
--	Decrements a value by 1
(type)	Casts a value to a specific type.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
LOGICAL COMPLEMENT AND NEGATION OPERATORS
Since we’re going to be working with a lot of numeric operators in this chapter, let’s get the boolean one out of the way first. The logical complement operator (!) flips the value of a boolean expression. For example, if the value is true, it will be converted to false, and vice versa.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Likewise, the negation operator, -, reverses the sign of a numeric expression
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
int pelican = !5;         // DOES NOT COMPILE
boolean penguin = -true;  // DOES NOT COMPILE
boolean peacock = !0;     // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Based on the description, it might be obvious that some operators require the variable or expression they’re acting upon to be of a specific type.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
INCREMENT AND DECREMENT OPERATORS
Increment and decrement operators, ++ and --, respectively, can be applied to numeric variables and have a high order of precedence, as compared to binary operators. In other words, they often get applied first in an expression.

Increment and decrement operators require special care because the order in which they are attached to their associated variable can make a difference in how an expression is processed. If the operator is placed before the operand, referred to as the pre-increment operator and the pre-decrement operator, then the operator is applied first and the value returned is the new value of the expression. Alternatively, if the operator is placed after the operand, referred to as the post-increment operator and the post-decrement operator, then the original value of the expression is returned, with operator applied after the value is returned.

The following code snippet illustrates this distinction:

int parkAttendance = 0;
System.out.println(parkAttendance);    // 0
System.out.println(++parkAttendance);  // 1
System.out.println(parkAttendance);    // 1
System.out.println(parkAttendance--);  // 1
System.out.println(parkAttendance);    // 0
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Working with Binary Arithmetic Operators
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Next, we move on to operators that take two arguments, called binary operators. Binary operators are by far the most common operators in the Java language. They can be used to perform mathematical operations on variables, create logical expressions, and perform basic variable assignments. Binary operators are often combined in complex expressions with other binary operators; therefore, operator precedence is very important in evaluating expressions containing binary operators.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Binary arithmetic operators

Operator	Description
+	Adds two numeric values
-	Subtracts two numeric values
*	Multiplies two numeric values
/	Divides one numeric value by another
%	Modulus operator returns the remainder after division of one numeric value by another
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
ARITHMETIC OPERATORS
Arithmetic operators are often encountered in early mathematics and include addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), division (/), and modulus (%).
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Adding Parentheses
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: That’s because you can change the order of operation explicitly by wrapping parentheses around the sections you want evaluated first.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Division and Modulus Operators
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The modulus operator, often called the remainder operator, is simply the remainder when two numbers are divided.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The modulus operation is not limited to positive integer values in Java; it may also be applied to negative integers and floating-point numbers. For example, if the divisor is 5, then the modulus value of a negative number is between -4 and 0.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: NUMERIC PROMOTION
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Numeric Promotion Rules
If two values have different data types, Java will automatically promote one of the values to the larger of the two data types.
If one of the values is integral and the other is floating-point, Java will automatically promote the integral value to the floating-point value’s data type.
Smaller data types, namely, byte, short, and char, are first promoted to int any time they’re used with a Java binary arithmetic operator, even if neither of the operands is int.
After all promotion has occurred and the operands have the same data type, the resulting value will have the same data type as its promoted operands.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: For the third rule, note that unary operators are excluded from this rule.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: For example, applying ++ to a short value results in a short value.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Java will automatically promote from smaller to larger data types
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: , but it will throw a compiler exception if it detects that you are trying to convert from larger to smaller data types without casting.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: CASTING VALUES
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Casting is a unary operation where one data type is explicitly interpreted as another data type. Casting is optional and unnecessary when converting to a larger or widening data type, but it is required when converting to a smaller or narrowing data type.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
float egg = 2.0 / 9;        // DOES NOT COMPILE
int tadpole = (int)5 * 2L;  // DOES NOT COMPILE
short frog = 3 - 2.0;       // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
int fish = 1.0;        // DOES NOT COMPILE
short bird = 1921222;  // DOES NOT COMPILE
int mammal = 9f;       // DOES NOT COMPILE
long reptile = 192301398193810323;  // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: OVERFLOW AND UNDERFLOW
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Overflow is when a number is so large that it will no longer fit within the data type, so the system “wraps around” to the lowest negative value and counts up from there, similar to how modulus arithmetic works. There’s also an analogous underflow, when the number is too low to fit in the data type, such as storing -200 in a byte field.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Last but not least, casting can appear anywhere in an expression, not just on the assignment.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
COMPOUND ASSIGNMENT OPERATORS
Besides the simple assignment operator (=) Java supports numerous compound assignment operators.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Compound assignment operators

Operator	Description
+=	Adds the value on the right to the variable on the left and assigns the sum to the variable
-=	Subtracts the value on the right from the variable on the left and assigns the difference to the variable
*=	Multiplies the value on the right with the variable on the left and assigns the product to the variable
/=	Divides the variable on the left by the value on the right and assigns the quotient to the variable
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: the compiler will automatically cast the resulting value to the data type of the value on the left side of the compound operator.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
ASSIGNMENT OPERATOR RETURN VALUE
One final thing to know about assignment operators is that the result of an assignment is an expression in and of itself, equal to the value of the assignment.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
EQUALITY OPERATORS
Determining equality in Java can be a nontrivial endeavor as there’s a semantic difference between “two objects are the same” and “two objects are equivalent.”
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Equality operators

Operator	Apply to primitives	Apply to objects
==	Returns true if the two values represent the same value	Returns true if the two values reference the same object
!=	Returns true if the two values represent different values	Returns true if the two values do not reference the same object
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
The equality operators are used in one of three scenarios:

Comparing two numeric or character primitive types. If the numeric values are of different data types, the values are automatically promoted. For example, 5 == 5.00 returns true since the left side is promoted to a double.
Comparing two boolean values
Comparing two objects, including null and String values
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
boolean monkey = true == 3;       // DOES NOT COMPILE
boolean ape = false != "Grape";   // DOES NOT COMPILE
boolean gorilla = 10.2 == "Koko"; // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: For object comparison, the equality operator is applied to the references to the objects, not the objects they point to. Two references are equal if and only if they point to the same object or both point to null.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
RELATIONAL OPERATORS
We now move on to relational operators, which compare two expressions and return a boolean value.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Relational operators

Operator	Description
<	Returns true if the value on the left is strictly less than the value on the right
<=	Returns true if the value on the left is less than or equal to the value on the right
>	Returns true if the value on the left is strictly greater than the value on the right
>=	Returns true if the value on the left is greater than or equal to the value on the right
a instanceof b	Returns true if the reference that a points to is an instance of a class, subclass, or class that implements a particular interface, as named in b
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Numeric Comparison Operators
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
int gibbonNumFeet = 2, wolfNumFeet = 4, ostrichNumFeet = 2;
System.out.println(gibbonNumFeet < wolfNumFeet);      // true
System.out.println(gibbonNumFeet <= wolfNumFeet);     // true
System.out.println(gibbonNumFeet >= ostrichNumFeet);  // true
System.out.println(gibbonNumFeet > ostrichNumFeet);   // false
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: instanceof Operator
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: It is common to use casting and instanceof together when working with objects that can be various different types, since it can give you access to fields available only in the more specific classes. It is considered a good coding practice to use the instanceof operator prior to casting from one object to a narrower type.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: null and the instanceof operator
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: calling instanceof on the null literal or a null reference always returns false.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: System.out.print(null instanceof null);  // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: LOGICAL OPERATORS
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The logical operators, (&), (|), and (^), may be applied to both numeric and boolean data types;
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: When they’re applied to boolean data types, they’re referred to as logical operators. Alternatively, when they’re applied to numeric data types, they’re referred to as bitwise operators, as they perform bitwise comparisons of the bits that compose the number.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Logical operators

Operator	Description
&	Logical AND is true only if both values are true.
|	Inclusive OR is true if at least one of the values is true.
^	Exclusive XOR is true only if one value is true and the other is false.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
AND is only true if both operands are true.
Inclusive OR is only false if both operands are false.
Exclusive OR is only true if the operands are different.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: SHORT-CIRCUIT OPERATORS
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Short-circuit operators

Operator	Description
&&	Short-circuit AND is true only if both values are true. If the left side is false, then the right side will not be evaluated.
||	Short-circuit OR is true if at least one of the values is true. If the left side is true, then the right side will not be evaluated.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The short-circuit operators are nearly identical to the logical operators, & and |, except that the right side of the expression may never be evaluated if the final result can be determined by the left side of the expression.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Avoiding a NullPointerException
A more common example of where short-circuit operators are used is checking for null objects before performing an operation.

highlighted_text: Switch Data Types
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Notice that boolean, long, float, and double are excluded from switch statements, as are their associated Boolean, Long, Float, and Double classes. The reasons are varied, such as boolean having too small a range of values and floating-point numbers having quite a wide range of values.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Prior to Java 5.0, this variable could only be int values or those values that could be promoted to int, specifically byte, short, char, or int, which we refer to as primitive numeric types.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
The following is a list of all data types supported by switch statements:

int and Integer
byte and Byte
short and Short
char and Character
String
enum values
var (if the type resolves to one of the preceding types)
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: There is no requirement that the case or default statement be in a particular order, unless you are going to have pathways that reach multiple sections of the switch block in a single execution.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Acceptable Case Values
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: First off, the values in each case statement must be compile-time constant values of the same data type as the switch value. This means you can use only literals, enum constants, or final constant variables of the same data type. By final constant, we mean that the variable must be marked with the final modifier and initialized with a literal value in the same expression in which it is declared.

highlighted_text: An array is an area of memory on the heap with space for a designated number of elements. A String is implemented as an array with some methods that you might want to use when dealing with characters specifically. A StringBuilder is implemented as an array where the array object is replaced with a new bigger array object when it runs out of space to store all the characters. A big difference is that an array can be of any other Java type.

highlighted_text:
Now that you know how to compare a single value, let’s look at how to compare arrays of different lengths:

If both arrays are the same length and have the same values in each spot in the same order, return zero.
If all the elements are the same but the second array has extra elements at the end, return a negative number.
If all the elements are the same but the first array has extra elements at the end, return a positive number.
If the first element that differs is smaller in the first array, return a negative number.
If the first element that differs is larger in the first array, return a positive number.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: what does smaller mean?
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
null is smaller than any other value.
For numbers, normal numeric order applies.
For strings, one is smaller if it is a prefix of another.
For strings/characters, numbers are smaller than letters.
For strings/characters, uppercase is smaller than lowercase.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Arrays.compare() examples

First array	Second array	Result	Reason
new int[] {1, 2}	new int[] {1}	Positive number	The first element is the same, but the first array is longer.
new int[] {1, 2}	new int[] {1, 2}	Zero	Exact match
new String[] {"a"}	new String[] {"aa"}	Negative number	The first element is a substring of the second.
new String[] {"a"}	new String[] {"A"}	Positive number	Uppercase is smaller than lowercase.
new String[] {"a"}	new String[] {null}	Positive number	null is smaller than a letter.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: When comparing two arrays, they must be the same array type.

Method	When arrays are the same	When arrays are different

mismatch()	-1	Zero or positive index

highlighted_text: Strings are immutable sequences of characters. The new operator is optional. The concatenation operator (+) creates a new String with the content of the first String followed by the content of the second String. If either operand involved in the + expression is a String, concatenation is used; otherwise, addition is used. String literals are stored in the string pool. The String class has many methods.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: StringBuilders are mutable sequences of characters.

highlighted_text: Calling == on String objects will check whether they point to the same object in the pool. Calling == on StringBuilder references will check whether they are pointing to the same StringBuilder object. Calling equals() on String objects will check whether the sequence of characters is the same. Calling equals() on StringBuilder objects will check whether they are pointing to the same object rather than looking at the values inside.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Functional programming is a way of writing code more declaratively. You specify what you want to do rather than dealing with the state of objects. You focus more on expressions than loops.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Functional programming uses lambda expressions to write code. A lambda expression is a block of code that gets passed around. You can think of a lambda expression as an unnamed method.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: It has parameters and a body just like full-fledged methods do, but it doesn’t have a name like a real method. Lambda expressions are often referred to as lambdas for short.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: a lambda expression is like a method that you can pass as if it were a variable.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Deferred execution means that code is specified now but will run later.

highlighted_text:
ACCESS MODIFIERS
Java offers four choices of access modifier:

private The private modifier means the method can be called only from within the same class.

Default (Package-Private) Access With default access, the method can be called only from classes in the same package. This one is tricky because there is no keyword for default access. You simply omit the access modifier.

protected The protected modifier means the method can be called only from classes in the same package or subclasses.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: public The public modifier means the method can be called from any class.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Encapsulation means only methods in the class with the variables can refer to the instance variables.

Applying the static Keyword
When the static keyword is applied to a variable, method, or class, it applies to the class rather than a specific instance of the class.

PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: You can use an instance of the object to call a static method. The compiler checks for the type of the reference and uses that instead of the object—which is sneaky of Java.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: STATIC VS. INSTANCE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: A static member cannot call an instance member without referencing an instance of the class. This shouldn’t be a surprise since static doesn’t require any instances of the class to even exist.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Passing Data among Methods
Java is a “pass-by-value” language. This means that a copy of the variable is made and the method receives that copy. Assignments made in the method do not affect the caller.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The variable s is a copy of the variable name. Both point to the same StringBuilder, which means that changes made to the StringBuilder are available to both references.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Overloading Methods
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Method overloading occurs when methods have the same name but different method signatures, which means they differ by method parameters.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Understanding Polymorphism
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Java supports polymorphism, the property of an object to take on many different forms.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: DEFINING A MEMBER INNER CLASS
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: A member inner class is a class defined at the member level of a class (the same level as the methods, instance variables, and constructors). It is the opposite of a top-level class, in that it cannot be declared unless it is inside another class.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Checked Exceptions
A checked exception is an exception that must be declared or handled by the application code where it is thrown. In Java, checked exceptions all inherit Exception but not RuntimeException.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Checked exceptions also include any class that inherits Throwable, but not Error or RuntimeException. For example, a class that directly extends Throwable would be a checked exception.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Because checked exceptions tend to be anticipated, Java enforces the rule that the programmer must do something to show the exception was thought about.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Unchecked Exceptions
An unchecked exception is any exception that does not need to be declared or handled by the application code where it is thrown. Unchecked exceptions are often referred to as runtime exceptions, although in Java, unchecked exceptions include any class that inherits RuntimeException or Error.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: A runtime exception is defined as the RuntimeException class and its subclasses. Runtime exceptions tend to be unexpected but not necessarily fatal.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
RUNTIME VS. AT THE TIME THE PROGRAM IS RUN
A runtime (unchecked) exception is a specific type of exception. All exceptions occur at the time that the program is run.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
RUNTIMEEXCEPTION CLASSES
RuntimeException and its subclasses are unchecked exceptions that don’t have to be handled or declared. They can be thrown by the programmer or by the JVM. Common RuntimeException classes include the following:

ArithmeticException Thrown when code attempts to divide by zero

ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException Thrown when code uses an illegal index to access an array

ClassCastException Thrown when an attempt is made to cast an object to a class of which it is not an instance

NullPointerException Thrown when there is a null reference where an object is required

IllegalArgumentException Thrown by the programmer to indicate that a method has been passed an illegal or inappropriate argument

NumberFormatException Subclass of IllegalArgumentException thrown when an attempt is made to convert a string to a numeric type but the string doesn’t have an appropriate format

ArithmeticException
Trying to divide an int by zero gives an undefined result. When this occurs, the JVM will throw an ArithmeticException:

int answer = 11 / 0;
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException:
Index -1 out of bounds for length 3
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
ClassCastException
Java tries to protect you from impossible casts. This code doesn’t compile because Integer is not a subclass of String:

String type = "moose";
Integer number = (Integer) type;  // DOES NOT COMPILE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ClassCastException:  java.base/java.lang.String
cannot be cast to java.lang.base/java.lang.Integer
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
NullPointerException
Instance variables and methods must be called on a non-null reference. If the reference is null, the JVM will throw a NullPointerException.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: IllegalArgumentException
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: NumberFormatException
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Java provides methods to convert strings to numbers. When these are passed an invalid value, they throw a NumberFormatException.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: In fact, NumberFormatException is a subclass of IllegalArgumentException.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Integer.parseInt("abc");
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Exception in thread "main"
java.lang.NumberFormatException: For input string: "abc"
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
CHECKED EXCEPTION CLASSES
Checked exceptions have Exception in their hierarchy but not RuntimeException.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
IOException Thrown programmatically when there’s a problem reading or writing a file

FileNotFoundException Subclass of IOException thrown programmatically when code tries to reference a file that does not exist
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
ERROR CLASSES
Errors are unchecked exceptions that extend the Error class. They are thrown by the JVM and should not be handled or declared.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
ExceptionInInitializerError Thrown when a static initializer throws an exception and doesn’t handle it

StackOverflowError Thrown when a method calls itself too many times (This is called infinite recursion because the method typically calls itself without end.)

NoClassDefFoundError Thrown when a class that the code uses is available at compile time but not runtime

ExceptionInInitializerError
Java runs static initializers the first time a class is used. If one of the static initializers throws an exception, Java can’t start using the class.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ExceptionInInitializerError
Caused by: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: -1 out of bounds for length 3
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The ExceptionInInitializerError is an error because Java failed to load the whole class. This failure prevents Java from continuing.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
StackOverflowError
When Java calls methods, it puts parameters and local variables on the stack. After doing this a very large number of times, the stack runs out of room and overflows. This is called a StackOverflowError
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Exception in thread "main" java.lang.StackOverflowError
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text:
NoClassDefFoundError
A NoClassDefFoundError occurs when Java can’t find the class at runtime. Generally, this means a library available when the code was compiled is not available when the code is executed.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: EXPLORING A MODULE
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: A module is a group of one or more packages plus a special file called module-info .java
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: BENEFITS OF MODULES
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Better Access Control
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: They can expose packages within the modular JAR to specific other packages. This stronger form of encapsulation really does create internal packages.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Clearer Dependency Management
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: In a fully modular environment, each of the open source projects would specify their dependencies in the module-info.java file. When launching the program, Java would complain that Hamcrest isn’t in the module path and you’d know right away.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Custom Java Builds
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The Java Platform Module System allows developers to specify what modules they actually need. This makes it possible to create a smaller runtime image that is customized to what the application needs and nothing more. Users can run that image without having Java installed at all.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: A tool called jlink is used to create this runtime image.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Improved Performance
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Since Java now knows which modules are required, it only needs to look at those at class loading time. This improves startup time for big programs and requires less memory to run.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Unique Package Enforcement
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: A package is only allowed to be supplied by one module. No more unpleasant surprises about a package at runtime.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: The jdeps command prints information about packages used in addition to module-level information. Finally, the jmod command is used when dealing with files that don’t meet the requirements for a JAR.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: Applying the final Modifier

highlighted_text: INTRODUCING ABSTRACT CLASSES
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: An abstract class is a class that cannot be instantiated and may contain abstract methods. An abstract method is a method that does not define an implementation when it is declared. Both abstract classes and abstract methods are denoted with the abstract modifier.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: There are some restrictions on the placement of the abstract modifier. The abstract modifier cannot be placed after the class keyword in a class declaration, nor after the return type in a method declaration.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I
PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

Chapter 10
Exceptions
OCP EXAM OBJECTIVES COVERED IN THIS CHAPTER:

Handling Exceptions
Describe the advantages of Exception handling and differentiate among checked, unchecked exceptions, and Errors

Create and invoke a method that throws an exception

highlighted_text:
Understanding Exceptions
A program can fail for just about any reason. Here are just a few possibilities:

The code tries to connect to a website, but the Internet connection is down.
You made a coding mistake and tried to access an invalid index in an array.
One method calls another with a value that the method doesn’t support.
As you can see, some of these are coding mistakes. Others are completely beyond your control. Your program can’t help it if the Internet connection goes down. What it can do is deal with the situation.
toc_family_titles: PART I: Exam 1Z0‐815, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer I

highlighted_text: the final modifier can be applied to variables, methods, and classes. Marking a variable final means the value cannot be changed after it is assigned. Marking a method or class final means it cannot be overridden or extended, respectively.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: Working with Enums
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: An enumeration is like a fixed set of constants. In Java, an enum, short for “enumerated type,” can be a top‐level type like a class or interface, as well as a nested type like an inner class.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: Using an enum is much better than using a bunch of constants because it provides type‐safe checking. With numeric or String constants, you can pass an invalid value and not find out until runtime. With enums, it is impossible to create an invalid enum value without introducing a compiler error.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11
PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11
PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: They can be compared using == because they are like static final constants. In other words, you can use equals() or == to compare enums, since each enum value is initialized only once in the Java Virtual Machine (JVM).
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11
PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: Another useful feature is retrieving an enum value from a String using the valueOf() method. This is helpful when working with older code. The String passed in must match the enum value exactly, though.

Season s = Season.valueOf("SUMMER"); // SUMMER
 
Season t = Season.valueOf("summer"); // Throws an exception at runtime
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: The immutable objects pattern is an object‐oriented design pattern in which an object cannot be modified after it is created. Instead of modifying an immutable object, you create a new object that contains any properties from the original object you want copied over.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: All enum constructors are implicitly private, with the modifier being optional. This is reasonable since you can't extend an enum and the constructors can be called only within the enum itself. An enum constructor will not compile if it contains a public or protected modifier.

highlighted_text: Creating Nested Classes
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text:
A nested class is a class that is defined within another class. A nested class can come in one of four flavors.

Inner class: A non‐ static type defined at the member level of a class
Static nested class: A static type defined at the member level of a class
Local class: A class defined within a method body
Anonymous class: A special case of a local class that does not have a name
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text:
DECLARING AN INNER CLASS
An inner class, also called a member inner class, is a non‐ static type defined at the member level of a class (the same level as the methods, instance variables, and constructors). Inner classes have the following properties:

Can be declared public, protected, package‐private (default), or private
Can extend any class and implement interfaces
Can be marked abstract or final
Cannot declare static fields or methods, except for static final fields
Can access members of the outer class including private members
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text:
.CLASS FILES FOR INNER CLASSES
Compiling the Outer.java class with which we have been working creates two class files.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: Understanding Interface Members
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text:
RELYING ON A DEFAULT INTERFACE METHOD
A default method is a method defined in an interface with the default keyword and includes a method body. Contrast default methods with abstract methods in an interface, which do not define a method body.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: A default method may be overridden by a class implementing the interface. The name default comes from the concept that it is viewed as an abstract interface method with a default implementation. The class has the option of overriding the default method, but if it does not, then the default implementation will be used.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text:
PURPOSE OF DEFAULT METHODS
One motivation for adding default methods to the Java language was for backward compatibility. A default method allows you to add a new method to an existing interface, without the need to modify older code that implements the interface.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: Another motivation for adding default methods to Java is for convenience. For instance, the Comparator interface includes a default reversed() method that returns a new Comparator in the order reversed.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11
PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text:
DEFAULT INTERFACE METHOD DEFINITION RULES
A default method may be declared only within an interface.
A default method must be marked with the default keyword and include a method body.
A default method is assumed to be public.
A default method cannot be marked abstract, final, or static.
A default method may be overridden by a class that implements the interface.
If a class inherits two or more default methods with the same method signature, then the class must override the method.

PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text:
These methods are defined explicitly with the static keyword and for the most part behave just like static methods defined in classes.

Static Interface Method Definition Rules

A static method must be marked with the static keyword and include a method body.
A static method without an access modifier is assumed to be public.
A static method cannot be marked abstract or final.
A static method is not inherited and cannot be accessed in a class implementing the interface without a reference to the interface name.
toc_family_titles: PART II: Exam 1Z0‐816, OCP Java SE 11 Programmer II Exam 1Z0‐817, Upgrade OCP Java SE 11

highlighted_text: Like default and abstract interface methods, static interface methods are implicitly public if they are declared without an access modifier.
